Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to find diagrams and charts, but with limited resources at home, I couldn't always access what I needed when I needed it. I'd usually cram in study sessions whenever I could find a few hours free, often late at night. There wasn't really a consistent schedule - more like bursts of intense focus followed by periods where I just couldn't bring myself to look at the material.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd study every day, other weeks barely at all. Deadlines were the main driver - I'd see something was due and suddenly find the motivation to put in the hours. Without that pressure, I'd often drift and procrastinate even though I knew I shouldn't.
Q3: Usually it was fear, to be honest. I'd get scared that I was falling behind or missing something important, and that anxiety would push me to log in and check what was happening. Sometimes I'd see a notification about an assignment deadline and panic would set in. Other times I'd just feel this nagging worry that everyone else was ahead of me.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to the point where logging in felt automatic or natural. Every time I accessed the LMS, it felt like a deliberate decision I had to push myself to make. The gamified tasks helped a bit because they made things feel less overwhelming, but I still couldn't make it a habit.
Q5: The video content was most useful for me because I'm a visual learner and seeing concepts explained helped them stick. I'd watch the lectures and try to visualize the concepts being discussed. The gamified tasks also helped because they broke things down into smaller, manageable pieces that didn't feel as intimidating as looking at the whole course at once.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums - I only posted once the entire course. I felt scared to put my thoughts out there and have people judge them. The readings I often skipped too because without good internet at home, downloading and reading long documents was frustrating. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but often didn't.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate results or when it connected clearly to what would be on the exam. The gamified elements helped because earning points or completing challenges gave me that instant feedback. If something felt abstract or disconnected from my grade, I'd struggle to prioritize it over everything else demanding my attention.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With limited time and resources, I had to focus on what was required just to pass. Required activities came with that fear of consequences if I didn't complete them. Optional ones, even if interesting, got pushed aside because the stress of mandatory work was always looming over me.
Q9: Absolutely. Once I downloaded materials, I'd study them offline constantly because my internet isn't reliable. I'd take screenshots of diagrams and study from my phone during commutes or breaks. I also made handwritten notes with visual maps and flowcharts that helped me understand connections between concepts better than just reading text.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened away from the LMS. I'd download a video once and watch it multiple times offline, pausing and rewinding to really understand. I'd sit with my notes for hours, drawing diagrams and testing myself. The LMS would only show that one download, but the real engagement was happening in my notebook and in my head.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then work with them offline for days or weeks. My internet situation made this necessary, but it also meant I could study whenever and wherever without worrying about connectivity. The LMS data would show minimal activity while I was actually deeply engaged with the content.
Q12: There were definitely times when the stress just paralyzed me. When assignments piled up and I felt behind, I'd get so scared that I'd avoid the LMS entirely because logging in meant confronting how much I hadn't done. The fear would build until a deadline forced me to act. It was a terrible cycle that I knew wasn't healthy but couldn't break.
Q13: Competing responsibilities hit me hard. With low resources, I sometimes had to prioritize work or family obligations over studying. Fatigue was constant because I'd stay up late trying to catch up. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Confusion about course material made me feel even more overwhelmed.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered to all the things stressing me out. I'd scroll through readings without absorbing anything because I was too tired or anxious to focus. My eyes were on the screen but my brain was somewhere else entirely, worrying about deadlines or other problems in my life.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they seemed difficult or when I was scared of doing poorly. The bigger assignments terrified me - I'd know they were important but the fear of failing would keep me from starting. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more prepared, but that day never really came until the deadline was breathing down my neck.
Q16: Deadlines controlled everything. Without a deadline, I'd drift and lose focus. With one approaching, I'd suddenly become intensely productive, sometimes pulling all-nighters to finish things. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that could overcome my fear and procrastination. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it gave me a boost of confidence that helped me engage more. My assignment scores were actually really high, which surprised me and made me feel like maybe I could do this. But when feedback was delayed or absent, I'd spiral into worry about whether I was on the right track. The gamified feedback was helpful because it was immediate.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to work toward, but the pace sometimes felt relentless. When I fell behind, the structure made it hard to catch up because new material kept coming while I was still struggling with old content. The gamified elements helped make the structure feel less rigid and more approachable.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier - they turned scary assignments into smaller challenges I could tackle. But the heavy reliance on online participation was harder for me given my internet situation. Discussion requirements stressed me out because I'm not confident sharing my thoughts publicly. More visual content and offline-friendly materials would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways - logging in out of fear or anxiety, clicking around trying to understand what's expected, but then getting overwhelmed and not actually completing the work. Or maybe they're engaging with content but struggling to translate that into finished assignments. High activity doesn't mean productive activity when you're stressed and scared.
Q21: This absolutely reflects my experience. My exam score was decent and I passed despite low LMS engagement because so much of my real studying happened offline. I'd download materials once and study them intensively away from the platform. The LMS couldn't see me reviewing my handwritten notes or redrawing diagrams until I understood them. Performance and visible activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and for how long, which gives a basic picture of engagement patterns. But they miss the quality of that engagement - whether you were really thinking or just going through the motions. They can't see offline study, emotional state, or the real understanding happening in your head. They show behavior but not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to avoid failing. It's when I'm curious and want to know more, when I'm making connections between concepts, when studying doesn't feel like a chore. For me, engagement happened most when I was working with visual materials offline, making my own diagrams and really wrestling with ideas.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how that fear both motivated and paralyzed me. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials, the visual notes I created, or the moments when concepts finally clicked. It wouldn't show that despite everything - the stress, the low resources, the anxiety - I actually cared about learning and worked hard in ways the system couldn't see.
